title: 8  GitHub Classroom
text:
9 Administering Coursework with GitHub Classroom
As you transition to a reproducible, version-controlled authoring workflow using Quarto and GitHub, managing student assignments becomes the next logical challenge. Traditional Learning Management Systems (LMS) like Blackboard are excellent for delivering content, but they can be cumbersome when distributing code templates, grading programming assignments, or managing student collaboration.
This is where GitHub Classroom shines. GitHub Classroom is a teaching tool designed to bridge the gap between professional version control and academic assignment management. By integrating GitHub Classroom into your course workflow, you can automate repository creation, distribute starter code seamlessly, and even implement autograding.

title: 8  GitHub Classroom
text:
9.1 Core Features for Educators
GitHub Classroom offers several powerful features that simplify the teaching and learning experience, making it the perfect companion for a Quarto-based course.

9.1.1 Template Repositories
The Quarto framework we established earlier involves generating a specific directory structure (M01, M02) with predefined lecture notes, labs, assignments, projects, presentations, and participation files (M01_A.qmd, M01_Proj.qmd, M01_Lab1.qmd). With GitHub Classroom, you can turn these generated directories into template repositories.
When you create an assignment, you can link it to your template repository. GitHub Classroom will then automatically generate a private, boilerplate repository for every student (or group) who accepts the assignment. This ensures every student starts with the exact same file structure and Quarto configurations.


9.1.2 Autograding
For courses that involve programming or reproducible data analysis, grading can be incredibly time-consuming. GitHub Classroom allows you to configure automated tests that run every time a student pushes a commit to their assignment repository.
Using GitHub Actions under the hood, these autograding scripts can check for syntax errors, verify that a Quarto document compiles correctly, or validate the output of a data analysis script.


9.1.3 Feedback Pull Requests
Instead of downloading zipped folders of student code from Blackboard, GitHub Classroom can automatically open a Feedback Pull Request in every student’s assignment repository. This allows you, or your teaching assistants, to leave line-by-line comments directly on the student’s code, mimicking a professional code review process.


9.1.4 LMS Integration
While we are using Blackboard Ultra as the primary content delivery platform, GitHub Classroom can connect directly to your LMS. By linking your Blackboard course to your GitHub Classroom, you can import your student roster directly. This ensures that every GitHub account is mapped to a specific student ID, preventing the common headache of figuring out which anonymous GitHub handle belongs to which student.

title: 8  GitHub Classroom
section: 9.2 Integrating Classroom with Your Quarto Workflow
text:
9.2 Integrating Classroom with Your Quarto Workflow
To implement GitHub Classroom effectively in this framework, consider the following workflow for each module:

Generate the Module: Use scripts/generate_modules.py to create the M01 folder and its M01_Lab1.qmd, M01_A.qmd, or M01_Proj.qmd files.
Author the Assignment: Write the assignment instructions and provide any starter data inside the data/ folder.
Create the Template: Push this module folder to a dedicated GitHub repository and mark it as a “Template Repository” in the repository settings.
Publish the Assignment: In GitHub Classroom, create a new assignment, select your template repository, and generate an invitation link.
Post to Blackboard: Finally, take the GitHub Classroom invitation link and embed it directly into your Blackboard Ultra learning module.

When students click the link in Blackboard, they will be taken to GitHub Classroom, where their personal, version-controlled Quarto workspace will be instantly generated. This workflow completely eliminates the friction of file distribution and empowers students to work in the same professional environments they will use in their careers.

title: 8  GitHub Classroom
section: 9.3 Integrating GitHub Classroom in an Analytics Class
text:
9.3 Integrating GitHub Classroom in an Analytics Class
In an analytics class, GitHub Classroom can serve as the distribution and collection layer for coding work. It can be used to create private repositories for each student or group, share starter Quarto, Python, or notebook files, and keep the assignment structure consistent across sections.
It also makes feedback easier. Students submit work through GitHub, and the instructor can review commits, compare revisions, and leave comments directly on files instead of managing multiple uploaded document versions through the LMS.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.1 Why Add a Cloud Workflow?
This chapter adapts the AD688 Module 0 cloud workflow into a reusable teaching pattern. The goal is not to turn every course into an AWS course. The goal is to give students a predictable way to work in a remote Linux environment, keep their work under version control, and produce reproducible Quarto deliverables.
Use this chapter when a course needs students to work with tools that are hard to install locally, require more compute than a laptop can provide, or benefit from a shared cloud environment.
A cloud workflow is useful when students need to:
For instructors, the cloud environment becomes a common baseline. When students say “it works on my machine,” the course has a shared machine to compare against.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
run Linux commands consistently across Windows, macOS, and Linux laptops
use the same Python, Jupyter, Quarto, or Spark environment
practice SSH and remote development
protect course work by pushing to GitHub frequently
learn the connection between local tools, cloud compute, and published outputs

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.2 The Operating Model
The AD688-style pattern connects four pieces:
flowchart LR
  A["GitHub repository"] --> B["Local editor"]
  B --> C["AWS EC2 instance"]
  C --> D["Jupyter or shell work"]
  D --> E["Quarto deliverable"]
  E --> A
Students begin with a GitHub repository, connect to a cloud machine, do their work in a controlled environment, render or prepare the deliverable, and push the result back to GitHub.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.3 Instructor Setup Checklist
Before assigning a cloud lab, confirm the pieces students will need.

AWS Academy course or approved AWS environment is available
Student access instructions have been distributed
A budget or usage limit is visible to students
Required instance type, operating system, and storage size are specified
SSH key instructions are clear for Windows, macOS, and Linux
Required VS Code extensions are listed
GitHub repository naming convention is defined
Submission format is explicit: repository URL, rendered file, screenshot, or LMS upload
Data files and starter code are reachable from the cloud environment

For AWS Academy Learner Lab courses, students should also read the learner lab guide and complete any required security or compliance modules before launching resources.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.4 Student Workflow
The student-facing sequence should stay short and repeatable.

Accept the AWS Academy or course cloud invitation.
Start the lab environment.
Launch an EC2 instance using the course-approved settings.
Download or locate the SSH private key.
Connect from VS Code Remote-SSH or a terminal.
Install or verify the required software.
Clone the assignment repository from GitHub.
Work in the repository.
Commit and push often.
Submit the required link or artifact.

This structure matters because cloud sessions can stop, instances can be restarted, and budgets can expire. GitHub is the durable record of student work.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.5 EC2 Launch Decisions
Give students exact choices instead of asking them to interpret the AWS console from scratch.







Decision
Course Guidance




Instance name
Use a predictable course name, such as ad688-lab01


Operating system
Use the course-approved Linux image


Instance type
Use the smallest approved type that supports the assignment


Key pair
Use the course-provided key pair or the required generated key


Network access
Allow only the ports required by the assignment


Storage
Use the course-approved size and avoid unnecessary volumes


Region or subnet
Keep all course resources in the same approved location



When students are new to cloud computing, the most important lesson is not “click every option.” It is “change only the settings the course asks you to change.”

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.6 SSH Key Handling
SSH keys deserve their own emphasis because mistakes here are common and risky.
Students should keep private keys:

out of GitHub
out of OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, and other synced folders
in a local .ssh folder or a course-specific secure folder
readable only by the user account that needs the key

On macOS, Linux, or WSL:
mv ~/Downloads/labsuser.pem ~/.ssh/vockey.pem
chmod 400 ~/.ssh/vockey.pem
Then connect with:
ssh -i ~/.ssh/vockey.pem ubuntu@PUBLIC_DNS_OR_IP
On Windows PowerShell, OpenSSH can also be used, but permissions sometimes need extra care:
icacls vockey.pem /inheritance:r
icacls vockey.pem /grant "$env:USERNAME:R"
ssh -i .\vockey.pem ubuntu@PUBLIC_DNS_OR_IP
For Windows students who are comfortable with Linux commands, WSL usually gives the smoothest SSH experience.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.7 VS Code Remote-SSH
VS Code Remote-SSH gives students a familiar editor while running commands and code on the EC2 instance.
Recommended extensions:

Remote - SSH
Python
Jupyter
Quarto
GitHub Pull Requests or GitHub integration tools

A simple SSH config entry looks like this:
Host course-ec2
  HostName PUBLIC_DNS_OR_IP
  User ubuntu
  IdentityFile ~/.ssh/vockey.pem
After this is saved, students can connect to course-ec2 from the Remote Explorer. Once connected, the terminal, file browser, Python interpreter, and Quarto commands run on the remote machine.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.8 Software Baseline
For an analytics course, the remote machine usually needs:

system package updates
Git
Python and pip
virtual environment support
Jupyter
Quarto
any course-specific libraries

A minimal Ubuntu setup is:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y git python3 python3-pip python3-venv
python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install jupyter pandas matplotlib
Then verify:
git --version
python --version
jupyter --version
quarto --version
If Quarto is not preinstalled, give students one course-approved installation method. Avoid asking beginners to mix package managers unless there is a clear reason.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.9 Working With GitHub From EC2
Students should clone their repository on the EC2 instance and push changes from there:
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/YOUR-REPOSITORY.git
cd YOUR-REPOSITORY
git status
During work:
git status
git add .
git commit -m "Complete cloud environment setup"
git push
If students use SSH remotes, their GitHub SSH key must be available on the EC2 instance. For many beginner labs, HTTPS authentication or GitHub CLI authentication is simpler to support.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.10 Running Jupyter Safely
When Jupyter is needed on EC2, prefer SSH port forwarding over opening broad public access.
From the local machine:
ssh -i ~/.ssh/vockey.pem -L 8000:localhost:8888 ubuntu@PUBLIC_DNS_OR_IP
On the EC2 instance:
jupyter notebook --no-browser --ip=127.0.0.1 --port=8888
Then open the forwarded local URL in the browser. This keeps the notebook server bound to the remote machine and accessed through the SSH tunnel.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.11 Using Quarto on EC2
Quarto fits naturally into the cloud workflow because the same source file can be edited remotely and rendered into a report, website, or slide deck.
Inside the repository:
quarto preview
quarto render
git status
git add .
git commit -m "Render Quarto deliverable"
git push
If a document depends on code execution, students should render it on the same environment where the required packages and data are available. This avoids the classic problem where a report renders locally but fails in the cloud, or the reverse.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.12 File Transfer
Most student work should move through GitHub. For one-off files, use scp from the local machine to copy a file from EC2:
scp -i ~/.ssh/vockey.pem ubuntu@PUBLIC_DNS_OR_IP:/home/ubuntu/path/to/file .
Students should run this from their own computer. Running scp from EC2 back to a laptop usually fails because the laptop is behind a home, campus, or public network that is not directly addressable.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.13 Budget and Shutdown Habits
Cloud labs add a professional responsibility: students must manage resources.
Recommended course policy:

check the lab budget before and after each work session
stop instances when finished
do not create extra resources unless the assignment requires them
push to GitHub before ending a session
never store secrets, private keys, access tokens, or passwords in the repository

For AWS Academy environments, remind students that budget displays can lag behind actual usage. The safe habit is to stop early, push often, and avoid unnecessary resources.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.14 Cloud Lab Template
Use this structure when writing a cloud lab.
# Lab Title

## Goal
What students will be able to do by the end.

## Required Environment
AWS Academy Learner Lab, EC2, VS Code Remote-SSH, GitHub repository.

## Setup
Start the lab, launch EC2, connect by SSH, clone the repository.

## Tasks
1. Verify the operating system and tools.
2. Install or activate the course environment.
3. Complete the analysis or build task.
4. Render the Quarto deliverable.
5. Commit and push.

## Deliverables
Repository URL, rendered HTML/PDF, command output, and reflection.

## Shutdown
Push work, stop the instance, confirm budget.

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.15 Completion Checklist

Cloud account or AWS Academy access works
EC2 instance launches with the required settings
SSH connection works
VS Code Remote-SSH connection works
GitHub repository is cloned on EC2
Python or course runtime is verified
Jupyter starts only through a safe access pattern
Quarto renders successfully
Work is committed and pushed
Instance is stopped when work is complete

title: 9  Cloud Analytics with AWS
text:
9.16 Instructor Notes
Start with one small cloud lab before using cloud infrastructure for a major assignment. The first lab should focus on access, SSH, GitHub, and rendering a tiny Quarto file. Once that workflow is reliable, later labs can focus on the analytics content instead of environment repair.
The best cloud assignment is boring at the infrastructure layer: students know where to click, where to type, where to save, and where to submit. That lets the course spend its energy on analysis, interpretation, and communication.

title: 10  Blackboard Ultra: Deploying Quarto Content to Your Course Shell
text:
10.1 Introduction
Blackboard Ultra is Boston University’s learning management system. It serves as the hub where students access your course materials, submit assignments, engage in discussions, and check their grades. The power of Blackboard Ultra emerges when you integrate it with your professionally-designed course materials created in Quarto.
This chapter guides you through the strategic integration of Quarto-generated course content with Blackboard Ultra’s content collection system. Rather than manually creating and updating content in Blackboard, you’ll develop your materials once in Quarto, generate HTML files, and deploy them as a complete package into Blackboard—making updates seamless and maintaining a single source of truth for your course materials.
By the end of this chapter, you’ll understand: - How Blackboard Ultra manages content and links to external resources - The workflow for packaging Quarto content into Blackboard-ready formats - The initial setup process with BU’s Blackboard team - The specific folder structure that enables seamless content management - How to deploy updates throughout the semester without disrupting your course

title: 10  Blackboard Ultra: Deploying Quarto Content to Your Course Shell
text:
10.2 Understanding Blackboard Ultra’s Architecture
Blackboard Ultra is structured around courses, modules, and content. Let’s clarify each:






Screenshot needed: Blackboard Ultra course home screen showing the top-level navigation — course banner, left sidebar, and content area.
Temporary reference: See the Blackboard Ultra overview on the Syracuse support page for a comparable layout.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-ultra-course-home.png




10.2.1 Courses: Your Course Shell
Your Blackboard course is the container for everything—all materials, assessments, discussions, and grades. Within Blackboard, you organize content into logical sections called modules.


10.2.2 Modules: Organizing Content into Units
Modules correspond to weeks, units, or thematic sections of your course. Each module typically contains lectures, readings, assignments, and discussions related to a specific topic.


10.2.3 Content Collection: The File System
Blackboard maintains a content collection—essentially a cloud file system where all your files are stored. Rather than embedding content directly in Blackboard’s interface, you upload files (HTML, PDFs, images) to the content collection and then link to them from various course elements.
This is where your Quarto-generated HTML files live. Once uploaded to the content collection, Blackboard displays them through its interface, maintaining consistent branding and navigation.






Screenshot needed: Blackboard content collection browser showing the folder hierarchy, upload button, and file listing with path breadcrumbs visible.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-content-collection-browser.png

title: 10  Blackboard Ultra: Deploying Quarto Content to Your Course Shell
section: 10.3 The Strategic Advantage: Quarto + Blackboard Integration
text:
10.3 The Strategic Advantage: Quarto + Blackboard Integration
Rather than creating content in Word or directly in Blackboard’s text editor, you’ll:

Author in Quarto: Create your course materials in clean, maintainable Quarto files (.qmd)
Generate HTML: Export everything as a complete website with quarto render
Package as _site.zip: Create a deployment-ready archive
Upload to Blackboard: Use Blackboard’s content collection to import the entire package
Link from Modules: Create course module structures that point to your HTML content
Update Seamlessly: When content changes, regenerate _site.zip and re-upload—Blackboard replaces old files with new versions


10.3.1 Why This Approach?
Single Source of Truth: Your course materials exist in one place (your Quarto project). Both you and your students work from the same, authoritative version.
Professional Presentation: Quarto produces clean, accessible HTML with consistent formatting. Your course looks polished without spending time on Blackboard’s styling.
Version Control: Your Quarto files live in Git, creating a complete history of how your course evolved.
Batch Updates: Fix typos, update examples, or revise content, then deploy all changes at once—not one file at a time.
Consistency Across Semesters: Template your course structure, then swap in new content for each term.

title: 10  Blackboard Ultra: Deploying Quarto Content to Your Course Shell
text:
10.4 Initial Setup: Before You Deploy

10.4.1 Critical First Step: Quarto Course Approval
Before you can effectively deploy Quarto content to Blackboard, your course structure must be officially approved at Boston University. This ensures compatibility with BU’s Blackboard configuration and technical support can assist if issues arise.
Process:

Email: kim.crosta@bu.edu
Purpose: Present your Quarto course structure and obtain approval
Timing: Do this before you finalize your module structure
Outcome: Kim will verify your approach aligns with BU standards

She may provide recommendations for optimization
You’ll receive guidance on any BU-specific requirements




10.4.2 Second Step: Structure Review with Andy
After Kim approves your Quarto approach, you’ll coordinate with Andy on the Blackboard side:

Detail your folder structure (see “Course Structure Template” below)
Discuss: Submission workflows and assignment deadlines

Trial Deployment

He’ll verify all links work correctly in Blackboard
You’ll test the student experience
Make adjustments before the semester starts




10.4.3 Knowledge Check: Setup Prerequisites
Before proceeding, verify you’ve completed the necessary groundwork:

You’ve created a preliminary Quarto project for your course
You’ve outlined your module structure (number of modules, topics)
You’ve contacted Kim Crosta and received approval

title: 10  Blackboard Ultra: Deploying Quarto Content to Your Course Shell
section: 10.5 Course Structure Template: The Full-Year Model
text:
10.5 Course Structure Template: The Full-Year Model
Now that you understand the setup process, let’s detail the specific structure you’ve developed for your courses. This structure balances content organization with manageable maintenance and clear student navigation.

10.5.1 Overview of Your Structure
Your model organizes each module around a core concept or unit, with distinct folders for content delivery, learning materials, and assignments:






Screenshot needed: Blackboard Ultra course content panel showing the full module list — all modules collapsed, so the reader can see the top-level naming convention (“Module 1 — Topic”, “Module 2 — Topic”, etc.).
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-module-list-overview.png



Module [Number] - [Topic Name]/
├── Wrap-Up Content (overview/summary file)
├── Highlight Page (special HTML content)
├── Module [X] Lecture 1/
│   ├── Lecture Notes
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Tutorial
├── Module [X] Lecture 2/
│   ├── Lecture Notes
│   ├── Presentation
│   └── Tutorial
└── Deliverables/
    ├── Assignment
    ├── Lab 1
    ├── Lab 2
    ├── Participation 1
    ├── Participation 2
    └── Project Step [N]


10.5.2 Understanding Each Component

10.5.2.1 Wrap-Up Content
Purpose: Provides a module overview, learning objectives, and synthesis of concepts.
Content Examples: - Summary of key concepts - Module learning outcomes - Prerequisites - Recommended reading order - Time estimates for completion
In Quarto: Create this as a dedicated .qmd file that renders as your module introduction. For example:
# Module 3: Advanced Data Visualization

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this module, you will:
- Understand principles of effective data visualization
- Create interactive visualizations with D3.js
- Apply color theory and accessibility standards

## Module Overview

This module explores how to transform raw data into compelling visual narratives...
In Blackboard: Link this as the first item in each module. Students see it immediately upon entering, setting context and expectations.






Screenshot needed: A single module expanded in Blackboard Ultra, with the Wrap-Up content item highlighted at the top — shows the “first item” position and the document icon.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-module-expanded-wrapup.png





10.5.2.2 Highlight Page
Purpose: A special HTML page highlighting a particular concept, real-world application, or student spotlight.
Examples: - “Industry Spotlight: How Netflix Uses Data Science” - “Case Study: Successful Project Implementation” - “Student Spotlight: Award-Winning Project” - “Emerging Trends: AI in Healthcare”
Creation: This can be a more visually-rich Quarto file, incorporating: - Images and diagrams - Embedded videos - Interactive elements - Special formatting or CSS customization
In Blackboard: Link this prominently in the module, perhaps as a featured item students should see.






Screenshot needed: The Highlight Page as it renders inside Blackboard’s content viewer — ideally showing a case study or industry spotlight with an image and formatted text.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-highlight-page-rendered.png





10.5.2.3 Lecture Folders (Module [X] Lecture 1, Module [X] Lecture 2)
Your course typically includes two lectures per module. Each lecture folder contains three standardized components:






Screenshot needed: One lecture folder expanded inside Blackboard Ultra, showing the three items — Lecture Notes, Presentation, and Tutorial — with their respective file icons.
Temporary reference: See Lesley University’s Blackboard Quick Start Guide for an example of content item layout inside a folder.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-lecture-folder-expanded.png




10.5.2.3.1 Lecture Notes
Format: Detailed written notes covering all lecture content.
In Quarto: Create as a .qmd file with:
# Lecture 1: Foundational Concepts

## What You'll Learn
- Core principle 1
- Core principle 2
- Core principle 3

## Detailed Notes

### Section 1: Background
Provide context and explain why this matters...

### Section 2: Methodology
Step-by-step explanation...

### Section 3: Examples
Real-world applications...

## Key Takeaways
- Point 1
- Point 2
- Point 3
Advantage: Students can review the lecture asynchronously, search for specific content, and have a reference document throughout the course.


10.5.2.3.2 Presentation
Format: The slide presentation from your live lecture or a structured presentation of key concepts.
In Quarto: Either: - Export slides as static HTML for viewing in the browser - Include links to PDF versions of presentations - Create interactive HTML presentations using Quarto’s reveal.js integration
Tip: Always provide both presentation and notes. Some students learn better from visuals; others prefer reading detailed explanations.


10.5.2.3.3 Tutorial
Format: Hands-on, step-by-step guidance for applying the lecture concepts.
In Quarto: Create interactive tutorials with: - Code examples (with syntax highlighting) - Expected outputs - Common mistakes to avoid - Practice exercises
Example structure:
# Tutorial: Building Your First Interactive Visualization

## Prerequisites
- Completed Lecture 1 and 2
- R or Python installed

## Step 1: Set Up Your Environment
your code here

## Step 2: Load Your Data
...

## Practice Exercise
Now you try! [Exercise prompt]

## Solution
[Revealed after students attempt]



10.5.2.4 Deliverables Folder
This folder organizes all student assignments, labs, participation activities, and project milestones. Each item has an associated submission window.






Screenshot needed: The Deliverables folder expanded in Blackboard Ultra, showing all six item types — Assignment, Lab 1, Lab 2, Participation 1, Participation 2, and Project Step — with submission due dates visible.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-deliverables-folder-expanded.png




10.5.2.4.1 Assignment
Purpose: The primary assessment for the module, typically demonstrating mastery of core concepts.
Structure: - Prompt or problem statement - Submission requirements - Grading rubric - Due date with submission window - Optional: Example solutions or rubrics
In Quarto: Create as a .qmd file with clear sections:
# Module 3 Assignment: Data Visualization Project

## Overview
Create an interactive visualization that tells a story with data.

## Requirements
1. ...
2. ...
3. ...

## Submission
- Format: HTML file or link to published visualization
- Due: [Date] by 11:59 PM ET
- Submit via Blackboard assignment tool

## Grading Rubric
[Detailed rubric with point allocations]

## Resources
- [Link to tutorial]
- [Link to examples]
- [Link to tool documentation]
In Blackboard: Set up a Blackboard Assignment with: - Same due date and submission window - Link to the assignment prompt (your HTML) - Grading rubric embedded or linked - Late submission policy






Screenshot needed: The Blackboard Ultra assignment creation screen showing the due date field, submission window settings, rubric attachment, and the content link field where the HTML prompt URL is pasted.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-assignment-creation-screen.png





10.5.2.4.2 Labs (2 per module)
Purpose: Shorter, focused practice activities reinforcing specific skills.
Structure: - Clear objective - Guided steps - Verification or self-check mechanism
Example Topics: - Lab 1: SQL Query Practice - Lab 2: Debugging Common Errors
Submission: Either in-Blackboard submission or link to external system with submission tracking.






Screenshot needed: A lab item open in Blackboard showing the submission area — the text box, file attachment option, and submit button the student sees.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-lab-submission-view.png





10.5.2.4.3 Participation Activities (2 per module)
Purpose: Engagement activities that demonstrate attendance and involvement. These might include:
Types: - Discussion board posts (peer feedback) - Reflection prompts (metacognitive activities) - Peer review assignments - Quiz-style quick checks - Live session engagement recording
Structure: - Clear prompt - Expected participation level (e.g., “1 main post + 2 peer responses”) - Grading criteria - Submission deadline
In Blackboard: Implement as: - Discussion board forums - Assignment submissions (for reflections) - Quiz tool (for quick checks) - Attendance tracking (for synchronous sessions)
Tip: Vary participation types throughout the semester to maintain engagement and accommodate different learning styles.






Screenshot needed: A Blackboard Ultra discussion board showing a participation prompt with student reply threads visible (blur student names if needed).
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-participation-discussion-board.png





10.5.2.4.4 Project Steps (progressive milestones)
Purpose: For courses with semester-long or multi-module projects, break them into manageable steps with intermediate deadlines.
Structure: - Project step number (e.g., “Project Step 1: Proposal”) - What students should submit - Deliverables - Feedback timeline - Submission window
Example Project Progression: - Step 1: Project Proposal (Module 1) - Step 2: Data Analysis & Exploration (Module 3) - Step 3: Preliminary Results & Visualization (Module 6) - Step 4: Final Report & Presentation (Module 8)
Advantage: Milestones prevent students from leaving everything until the last minute and give you multiple checkpoints to provide feedback.






Screenshot needed: Project step submission in Blackboard showing the milestone title, point value, due date, and the submission window with “Opens” and “Due” dates both visible.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-project-step-submission-window.png







10.5.3 Workbook Exercise: Design Your Module Structure
Take time to plan your course structure before building it in Quarto and Blackboard.
Task: Map out your course modules and components.
Step 1: Decide how many modules you’ll have and what each covers
Module 1: _________________________________
Module 2: _________________________________
Module 3: _________________________________
[Continue for all modules]
Step 2: For each module, plan the components
Module 1: [Topic Name]
├── Wrap-Up Content: [What will you overview?]
├── Highlight Page: [What special content/case study?]
├── Lecture 1: ___________________________
├── Lecture 2: ___________________________
├── Assignment: __________________________
├── Lab 1: ________________________________
├── Lab 2: ________________________________
├── Participation 1: _____________________
├── Participation 2: _____________________
└── Project Step: _________________________
Step 3: Count total deliverables

Number of lectures: _____
Number of assignments: _____
Number of labs: _____
Number of participation activities: _____
Number of project steps: _____

Verification Checklist: - [ ] All modules have defined content - [ ] Each module follows the consistent structure - [ ] Deliverable count is realistic for your course load - [ ] You have feedback capacity for all assessments - [ ] Project steps align with semester timeline (if applicable)

section: 10.6 Creating Content in Quarto for Blackboard Deployment
text:
10.6 Creating Content in Quarto for Blackboard Deployment
Now that you have your structure planned, let’s set up your Quarto project to generate deployable content.

10.6.1 Project Organization in Quarto
Create a Quarto project with folders that mirror your Blackboard structure:






Screenshot needed: VS Code or your editor showing the Quarto project folder tree — all module-01/ through module-N/ folders open with the subfolders lecture-1/, lecture-2/, and deliverables/ visible.
Replace with: images/ch07/quarto-project-folder-structure.png



your-course/
├── _quarto.yml
├── README.md
├── index.qmd
├── module-01/
│   ├── _module01.yml
│   ├── wrap-up.qmd
│   ├── highlight.qmd
│   ├── lecture-1/
│   │   ├── lecture-notes.qmd
│   │   ├── presentation.qmd
│   │   └── tutorial.qmd
│   ├── lecture-2/
│   │   ├── lecture-notes.qmd
│   │   ├── presentation.qmd
│   │   └── tutorial.qmd
│   └── deliverables/
│       ├── assignment.qmd
│       ├── lab-1.qmd
│       ├── lab-2.qmd
│       ├── participation-1.qmd
│       ├── participation-2.qmd
│       └── project-step-1.qmd
├── module-02/
│   └── [same structure]
└── images/
    ├── module-01/
    ├── module-02/
    └── [shared images]


10.6.2 Configuration: _quarto.yml
Set up your _quarto.yml to generate a website structure compatible with Blackboard:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: _site
  render:
    - "*.qmd"
    - "*/**.qmd"

website:
  title: "Your Course Title"
  site-url: "https://pages.github.com/your-course"
  repo-url: "https://github.com/your-org/your-course"
  repo-actions: [edit, issue, source]
  
  navbar:
    background: primary
    search: true
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - text: Course Modules
        menu:
          - href: module-01/wrap-up.qmd
            text: "Module 1"
          - href: module-02/wrap-up.qmd
            text: "Module 2"
          - text: "---"
          - text: Resources
          - href: resources.qmd
            text: Course Resources

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    css: custom.css
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-copy: true


10.6.3 Rendering and Packaging for Blackboard
When you’re ready to deploy content to Blackboard:






Screenshot needed: Terminal window showing the output of quarto render completing successfully — progress indicators, file counts, and the final “Output created: _site/” line.
Replace with: images/ch07/quarto-render-terminal-output.png



# Render all Quarto files to HTML
quarto render

# This creates the _site/ folder with all HTML, CSS, images, etc.
# Now package it for Blackboard upload
Creating _site.zip:
# On Windows (PowerShell)
Compress-Archive -Path _site -DestinationPath _site.zip

# On macOS/Linux
zip -r _site.zip _site/


10.6.4 Image References: Why They Persist
One important note about image links: When you reference images in your Quarto files and they’re included in _site.zip, Blackboard will import them along with the HTML. This means your image links will continue to work because:

Relative paths: If your HTML files reference images with relative paths (e.g., ../images/diagram.png), the image hierarchy is preserved
Content collection structure: Blackboard maintains the folder structure when you import _site.zip
Automatic linking: Blackboard’s content collection system updates internal references automatically

Important: Always use relative paths in your Quarto files, not absolute URLs:
❌ Don’t do this:
![Diagram](https://example.com/images/diagram.png)
✓ Do this:
![Diagram](../images/diagram.png)

10.7 Uploading Content to Blackboard

10.7.1 Initial Setup: First-Time Upload
For your first deployment (after approval from Kim and orientation from Andy):






Screenshot needed: File Explorer (Windows) or Finder (macOS) showing the _site/ folder and the generated _site.zip file side by side, with file sizes visible.

Generate _site.zip

Compress-Archive -Path _site -DestinationPath _site.zip
Access Blackboard Content Collection

Log into your Blackboard course
Go to Course Management → Content Collection
Create a folder structure mirroring your modules (or follow Andy’s guidance)








Screenshot needed: Blackboard Ultra admin panel showing the path Course Management → Content Collection, with the navigation breadcrumb and folder view visible.
Temporary reference: See Syracuse’s Blackboard Ultra guide for a comparable content collection view.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-content-collection-nav.png

Click Upload Files
Select _site.zip
Blackboard extracts the contents and maintains folder structure








Screenshot needed: The Blackboard “Upload Files” dialog — showing the file picker with _site.zip selected and the progress/confirmation screen after upload.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-upload-site-zip-dialog.png




Create Module Links

Navigate to each course module in Blackboard
For each content piece, create a link pointing to the corresponding HTML file in the content collection
Example: Module 1 → Click to link → navigate to content-collection/_site/module-01/wrap-up.html








Screenshot needed (2 images):

4a — The “Add Content” menu in Blackboard Ultra, with “Content Collection Item” or “Web Link” option highlighted.
4b — The file picker navigating the content collection to _site/module-01/wrap-up.html, with the file selected.

Replace with:
  images/ch07/bb-add-content-menu.png
  images/ch07/bb-content-collection-file-picker.png




Test All Links

Click through every link as a student would
Verify images display
Verify any embedded videos or interactive content work
Test on multiple browsers

Review with Andy

Demonstrate the working course to Andy
Make any adjustments he recommends
Get approval before launching to students




10.7.2 Subsequent Updates: Semester Deployment
After your initial setup, updating content becomes straightforward:






Screenshot needed: Side-by-side of the content collection folder before and after re-uploading _site.zip — showing the “Date Modified” column updating to the new timestamp while links in Blackboard remain unchanged.
Replace with: images/ch07/bb-content-collection-before-after-update.png




Make Changes in Quarto
Edit .qmd files as needed
Render and Package
quarto render
Compress-Archive -Path _site -DestinationPath _site.zip
Upload New _site.zip

Go to Blackboard Content Collection
Choose Upload Files and select your new _site.zip
Blackboard replaces old files with updated versions
Existing links automatically point to the updated content


Important Caveat: This replacement process works perfectly for content files (lectures, tutorials, materials). However, if you’re changing your module structure (adding/removing modules, reordering sections), you’ll need to create new links or adjust existing ones in Blackboard. This is why the initial setup with Andy is critical—lock in your structure before the semester starts.


10.7.3 Workbook Exercise: Prepare Your First Deployment
Task: Package your Quarto content for deployment.
Step 1: Verify your Quarto project structure
# Navigate to your course folder
cd /path/to/your-course

# List contents to verify structure
Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Filter "*.qmd" | Select-Object FullName
Step 2: Render all content
# Render your Quarto project
quarto render

# Verify _site folder was created
Test-Path _site
Step 3: Create deployment package
# Create _site.zip
Compress-Archive -Path _site -DestinationPath _site.zip -Force

# Verify the archive was created
Get-Item _site.zip | Select-Object Name, Length
Step 4: Prepare deployment checklist

Quarto project renders without errors
All .qmd files convert to .html successfully
Images and assets included in _site/
_site.zip created successfully
File size is reasonable (typically 50MB-500MB depending on content)
All internal links use relative paths (not absolute URLs)

Step 5: Documentation for Andy
Create a brief document for your Blackboard administrator:

Course: _________________________________ Number of modules: _____ Total files in package: _____ Approximate size: _____ Deployment date: _________________________________ Special requirements or notes: _________________________________

section: 10.8 Best Practices for Quarto + Blackboard Integration
text:
10.8 Best Practices for Quarto + Blackboard Integration

10.8.1 File Naming Conventions
Use clear, consistent naming that makes sense in both Quarto file systems and Blackboard:
✓ Good: - module-01-intro.qmd - lecture-1-foundations.qmd - assignment-project-proposal.qmd - lab-01-sql-basics.qmd
❌ Avoid: - module1.qmd - lecture.qmd - assignment.qmd - lab.qmd


10.8.2 URL/Link Strategy
When creating cross-links in your course materials:
Linking within Blackboard: Use relative paths that Blackboard can resolve:
See [this tutorial](../lecture-1/tutorial.html)
Linking to external resources: Use full URLs:
Check out [this guide](https://example.com/guide)
Linking to assignments: Reference Blackboard assignment links:
Submit your work via [this Blackboard assignment link](https://blackboard.bu.edu/webapps/assignment/...)


10.8.3 Maintenance During the Semester
Content Updates Without Redeployment: - Small text corrections: Fix in Quarto, render, and re-upload _site.zip - Broken links: Test regularly, fix in Quarto, re-render - Typos in assignments: Fix immediately and update within 24 hours
When NOT to Re-upload _site.zip: - After students have already submitted work on an assignment (the submission date might change if Blackboard refreshes) - If you’re only updating one file (upload it individually instead) - During peak submission periods (upload during off-hours to avoid conflicts)
Communication with Students: - If you make significant content changes, post an announcement: “Updated Module 3 lecture notes—please review the latest version” - Include version numbers in assignment prompts: “Submit your response to Assignment 3 (v1.1)”


10.8.4 Disaster Recovery and Rollback
Because your Quarto files are in version control (Git):

Keep previous deployments: Don’t delete old _site.zip files
Tag stable versions: In Git, tag releases after successful deployments
git tag -a v1.0-spring-2026 -m "Spring 2026 course - first deployment"
Rollback if needed: If something breaks, restore from the previous version:
git checkout v1.0-spring-2026
quarto render
Compress-Archive -Path _site -DestinationPath _site.zip
# Upload the previous version to Blackboard

title: 10  Blackboard Ultra: Deploying Quarto Content to Your Course Shell
text:
10.11 Troubleshooting Common Issues

10.11.1 Problem: Links Break After Uploading to Blackboard
Cause: Absolute URLs in Quarto files or incorrect relative paths
Solution: - Check all image and link references in your .qmd files - Use relative paths: ../images/logo.png not /images/logo.png - Update any absolute URLs to relative paths - Re-render and re-upload


10.11.2 Problem: Images Don’t Display in Blackboard
Cause: Images not included in _site/ folder or path references are wrong
Solution: - Ensure images are in a subfolder of your Quarto project (e.g., images/) - Verify Quarto rendering includes images in _site/ - Check that _site.zip includes image files - Verify relative paths in HTML match actual image locations


10.11.3 Problem: Content Collection Upload Fails
Cause: File size too large or connection interrupted
Solution: - If _site.zip is very large (>1GB), compress images bash   # Reduce image quality in Quarto with CSS   img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; } - Try uploading individual files instead of _site.zip - Contact Andy if technical issues persist


10.11.4 Problem: Students See Old Content After Re-upload
Cause: Browser caching
Solution: - Instruct students to clear browser cache or use Ctrl+Shift+Delete - Use browser-specific cache-busting in _quarto.yml - Announce updates in course announcements


10.11.5 Problem: Module Structure Changes Required Mid-Semester
Cause: Didn’t lock structure with Andy before launch
Solution: - Contact Andy before making structural changes - Update both Quarto and Blackboard simultaneously - Communicate changes to students - Test thoroughly before making live

title: 11  Automating with AI
text:
12 Supercharging Course Production with GitHub Copilot & AI Tools
As an educator building out reproducible courses with Quarto, you are inherently writing code. Whether it is Markdown for lecture notes, Python or R for your data analysis labs, or simply writing Python scripts to automate your directory structures, you are functioning as a developer.
To assist you in this endeavor, GitHub Copilot serves as an indispensable AI pair programmer. Copilot is integrated directly into your IDE (like Antigravity or VS Code) and provides real-time autocomplete suggestions, conversational assistance, and code generation.

title: 11  Automating with AI
text:
12.1 The AI Editor Stack
For day-to-day course production, the most practical setup is a combination of:

GitHub for repositories, version history, and publishing
GitHub Copilot for inline suggestions and chat support while writing code or Quarto
GitHub Classroom for distributing coding assignments and collecting student work
VS Code or Antigravity when using ChatGPT-connected coding tools for drafting scripts, revising content, and debugging
Cursor when project-wide AI editing and rapid refactoring are useful
Gemini and OpenAI APIs when you want scripts to generate metadata, summaries, or structured course assets at scale

Used together, these tools reduce setup time, help standardize materials, and make it easier to maintain course content across semesters.

title: 11  Automating with AI
text:
12.2 Additional Platforms and Resources
The following tools are also useful in analytics and AI-focused teaching workflows:

Remotion for generating videos programmatically from code: https://www.remotion.dev/
Manim for mathematical and technical animations in Python: https://docs.manim.community/en/stable/examples.html
Hugging Face Inference Providers for testing and comparing hosted models and APIs: https://huggingface.co/inference/models
Hugging Face Academia Hub for institution-level research, teaching, and infrastructure workflows: https://huggingface.co/docs/hub/en/academia-hub

title: 11  Automating with AI
text:
12.3 Copilot for Course Organization Scripts
One of the most powerful uses of Copilot is automating the tedious parts of course administration.
For instance, consider the requirement of generating a consistent module structure (M01, M02, M03) complete with boilerplate Quarto files for lecture notes, presentations, labs, assignments, projects, and participation activities. Instead of creating each file by hand, you can use the generator script included with this book.
If you want Copilot to help you adapt the script, give it this prompt:

“Modify this Quarto course scaffold script so it generates modules named M01 through M07. Each module should include M01_A.qmd, M01_LN1.qmd, M01_LN2.qmd, M01_P1.qmd, M01_P2.qmd, M01_Proj.qmd, M01_Lab1.qmd, M01_Lab2.qmd, M01_Participation1.qmd, and M01_Participation2.qmd.”


12.3.1 The Module Generation Script
The reusable version of the script is included in this repository at scripts/generate_modules.py. Run it from the root of a course project:
python scripts/generate_modules.py .
By default, the script creates modules M01 through M07. If your course has an orientation module, add M00:
python scripts/generate_modules.py . --include-module-zero
The first generated module will look like this:
M01/
  M01_A.qmd
  M01_LN1.qmd
  M01_LN2.qmd
  M01_P1.qmd
  M01_P2.qmd
  M01_Proj.qmd
  M01_Lab1.qmd
  M01_Lab2.qmd
  M01_Participation1.qmd
  M01_Participation2.qmd
  data/
  output/
  M01_lecture01_figures/
  M01_lecture02_figures/
  M01_presentation01_files/
  M01_presentation02_files/
  M01_P1_files/
  M01_P2_files/
Existing files are left untouched unless you explicitly pass --overwrite.


12.3.2 Validating the Module Structure
After generating or editing the scaffold, run the validation script:
python scripts/validate_course_structure.py .
The validator checks that every module contains the required M01_* files and support folders. This is useful before building Blackboard content because broken naming conventions often become broken links later.

title: 11  Automating with AI
text:
12.4 Automating Metadata with LLMs (OpenAI / Gemini)
Beyond structural generation, you can use large language models (LLMs) via APIs to automatically populate the YAML metadata in your Quarto files. For example, a script can read your course outline (an Excel or CSV file) and prompt an AI to generate SEO-friendly keywords and summaries for each lecture note.

12.4.1 Setting up API Keys (.env)
Before running API-dependent scripts, you must securely store your API keys. Never hardcode your keys into your Python scripts or commit them to GitHub. Instead, use a .env file.
Create a file named .env in the root of your project directory and add the following placeholder keys. Replace your_... with your actual keys.
# .env

# OpenAI API Key (Required for OpenAI integrations)
OPENAI_API_KEY="your_openai_api_key_here"

# Gemini API Key (Required if you use Google's models instead)
GEMINI_API_KEY="your_gemini_api_key_here"






Warning



Ensure your .gitignore file contains the line .env so your keys are not accidentally uploaded to your GitHub repository!




12.4.2 The Metadata Population Script
Once your keys are configured, use the included metadata script to fill YAML front matter in generated .qmd files from a CSV outline:
python scripts/populate_qmd_metadata.py data/generated_schedule.csv --root .
The script reads Source File, Title, Lecture, and Description columns. If OPENAI_API_KEY is present, it asks the OpenAI API for a concise page description. If no key is present, it falls back to the workbook description, so the workflow still works offline.

text:
12.5 Additional Automation Scripts
The book repository includes these reusable scripts:







Script
Purpose

scripts/validate_course_workbook.py

scripts/generate_modules.py

Together, these scripts turn the course-building workflow into a repeatable pipeline: workbook, structure, schedule, metadata, render, Blackboard.

title: 11  Automating with AI
text:
12.6 Copilot for Authoring Quarto Content
Copilot is not just for Python scripts. Because Quarto is a text-based format (.qmd), Copilot understands the context of your lecture notes and can predict what you want to write next.

Generating Code Blocks: If you are writing a lab assignment and need to insert a code block showing how to plot a scatterplot using matplotlib or ggplot2, simply write the comment # create a scatterplot of x vs y and Copilot will suggest the exact code block.
Writing Summaries: You can highlight a large chunk of text in your lecture notes and ask Copilot Chat to “Summarize this section for the introduction.”
Translating Content: If you are moving an old syllabus from Word to Quarto Markdown, you can prompt Copilot to convert your plain text into structured Markdown tables and formatted lists.

title: 11  Automating with AI
text:
12.7 Responsible AI Use in Education
While GitHub Copilot is a powerful authoring assistant for you as an educator, it also introduces important discussions regarding responsible use in the classroom.
If you are using GitHub Classroom (discussed in Chapter 6), your students may also have access to Copilot through GitHub Education. Verified students can get free access to GitHub Copilot, and Copilot Chat can be used with repository context to answer questions about the codebase. It is critical to establish a clear policy in your syllabus regarding the acceptable use of Generative AI.






Tip



Use Copilot to help you generate boilerplate code, format markdown, and troubleshoot errors. However, always review the generated content critically before publishing it to your Blackboard course shell to ensure pedagogical accuracy.


Integrating AI into your workflow does not replace your expertise; it simply accelerates the mechanics of course production, giving you more time to focus on teaching.

title: 11  Automating with AI
section: 12.8 Integrating These Tools in an Analytics Class
text:
12.8 Integrating These Tools in an Analytics Class
In an analytics class, these tools can speed up the parts of course design that are repetitive but still technical. GitHub and GitHub Classroom help organize materials, distribute assignments, and keep submissions structured. GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and ChatGPT-connected coding tools help draft scripts, clean up Quarto files, troubleshoot issues, and generate boilerplate more quickly.
For larger automation tasks, OpenAI or Gemini APIs can be used from Python scripts to generate metadata, summaries, or other structured content. Remotion and Manim can support technical video creation, while Hugging Face can support model experimentation or institution-scale AI workflows. The main value is not replacing instructional judgment, but reducing the time spent on setup, formatting, and iteration.

12.3 Step 1: Start From GitHub
Create the GitHub repository first, then clone it locally:
git clone https://github.com/your-org/mini-course.git

Initialize Quarto inside the cloned repository:
quarto create project website .
git add .
git commit -m "Initialize Quarto course site"
git push
This sequence keeps the course source of truth on GitHub from the beginning.

title: 13  Summary
text:
This book has walked through the full course-production workflow:

setting up the authoring environment
managing course materials with Git and GitHub
writing reproducible documents with Quarto
designing the course feeder workbook
building a Quarto microsite with a consistent M01_* module structure
using GitHub Classroom for code-based coursework
preparing content for Blackboard Ultra
automating repetitive course-building tasks with scripts and AI-assisted tools

The goal is not just to create one attractive course website. The goal is to give professors a repeatable system they can reuse across semesters, sections, and courses.
The recommended next step is to build a small pilot course using the same structure: one syllabus page, one schedule page, one deliverables page, and one complete module folder. Once that pilot renders cleanly and maps into Blackboard Ultra, the same pattern can scale to the full course.
